title: Auto Loan Balances Increase for Ninth Straight Quarter, New York Fed Report Shows

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: The New York Fed's Q2 2013 Household Debt and Credit Report showed that total auto loan balances increased $20 billion from the previous quarter, marking the ninth consecutive quarterly increase and the largest quarter-over-quarter increase since 2006. Auto loan originations reached $92 billion, the highest level since Q3 2007. Total household indebtedness fell to $11.15 trillion, 0.7% lower than the previous quarter and 12% below the Q3 2008 peak of $12.68 trillion. Delinquency rates declined across all household debt categories.
key_points: Auto loan balances increased $20 billion in Q2 2013, the ninth consecutive quarterly increase and largest quarter-over-quarter increase since 2006
Total auto loan balances crossed the $800 billion mark for the first time since Q3 2008
Auto loan originations reached $92 billion in Q2 2013, the highest level since Q3 2007
90+ day delinquent auto loan balance fell to 3.6%, the lowest level in five years
Total household indebtedness fell to $11.15 trillion, 12% below the Q3 2008 peak
Overall 90+ day delinquency rate for all household debt declined to 5.7% from 6.1% in Q1 2013
why_it_matters: This report tracks consumer credit health and debt composition, which matters for understanding household sector deleveraging and credit conditions. Rising auto loan balances may indicate consumer willingness to take on non-housing debt, while declining delinquency rates suggest improving household financial health.
macro_context: The report provided a quarterly snapshot of U.S. household debt trends in mid-2013, documenting the ongoing deleveraging cycle following the financial crisis. Rising auto loan debt represented an exception to the broader decline in housing-related debt.
important_numbers: $800 billion total auto loan balance threshold
5.7% overall 90+ day household debt delinquency rate

issues_found: Source published 2013-08-14, approximately 12.5 years before TODAY (2026-04-10), well beyond the 90-day threshold; however, the source has clear archival value as historical documentation of post-crisis household deleveraging trends
